title: Chapter 15: 25M: The Sunset Deduction
content: The sky is doing that thing again.

Streaks of amber and rose and burning gold, the whole western horizon like someone spilled a bottle of expensive cosmetics across the atmosphere. Manhattan is silhouetted against it — cranes and spires and glass catching fire in the last light. It is, by any objective measure, one of the most beautiful sunsets I've ever seen.
I know this because I went to art school.

"Chloe, give me wonder," Maren calls from behind the camera. She's the photographer — forties, silver-streaked undercut, the kind of eye that could make a brick wall look aspirational. "One more, baby. Wonder."

I widen my eyes slightly. Tilt my chin up. Part my lips a fraction. The wind kicks up and moves my hair across my face in exactly the way the creative director briefed. The shutter clicks in bursts of three.
"Beautiful," Maren says. But she's not looking at me anymore. She's looking at the sky.
She lowers her camera.

The grip — big guy, neck tattoo, has spent the entire shoot adjusting a C-stand with the focus of a neurosurgeon — he stops too. Just stands there with his sandbag in hand, staring west. The PA pauses mid-step, clipboard against her chest, mouth open. The makeup artist comes out from behind the reflector and just... stops.
Everyone on this rooftop is freezing the same moment. Holding their breath. Moved.
And I'm standing here in a twenty-thousand-dollar couture slip dress, holding a perfume bottle that costs more than my first apartment's annual rent, and what I feel is—
Technically impressed.

The colors are objectively beautiful. The composition would make a Renaissance painter weep. The way the light renders the skyline is aesthetically optimal. I know all of this the way I know that water is wet and the Mets will break your heart. It's information. It registers.
It doesn't land.
Maren wipes her eye with the back of her hand. "Sorry," she laughs, embarrassed. "I'm a sucker for golden hour. Gets me every time."
I smile. My cheek muscles perform the correct sequence. "It really is something," I say.

The words are hollow. I don't think anyone notices. That's the worst part — or it would be, if I could feel "worst" anymore. I'm so good at performing the shape of feeling that no one can see the void underneath. I'm a special effect. A practical one.
My phone vibrates in the pocket of the silk robe they gave me between setups. One pulse. Two. That now-familiar rhythm, like a heartbeat that belongs to something that doesn't have one.
"I need the bathroom," I tell the PA.

She nods without looking up — she's still gazing at the sky, the poor thing. I walk across the rooftop to the porta-john the production company rented, close the door behind me, and sit on the closed lid in the dark.
I pull out my phone.
The notification fills the screen in that clean, sans-serif font that Mephisto uses — the typographic equivalent of a doctor's handwriting, all professional detachment:
**Congratulations, Creator. 25,000,000 followers reached.**
**Deduction: Nostalgia.**
**Processing...**
I watch the wheel spin for exactly four seconds. Then it resolves:
**Deduction complete. Nostalgia removed from emotional palette.**
**Thank you for your continued partnership.**
I stare at the words. Nostalgia. The warm ache. The bittersweet. The thing that made the past feel like it mattered, that made memory more than metadata.
Gone.

I don't scream. I don't cry. I sit in a plastic porta-john on a Midtown rooftop in a couture dress and I feel the space where nostalgia used to live — and the space is just a space. Like a room you walk into and can't remember why you entered.
I put my phone away. I fix my hair in the tiny mirror. I go back to the shoot.
Because that's what influencers do. We perform.

The perfume ad wraps at 7:43 p.m. I take the town car home. The driver asks if I want music. I say no. The city slides past the windows in streaks of brake light and neon, and I watch it the way you'd watch a screen saver.

Leo's already home when I walk in. He's at his desk in the second bedroom — the one we call "the office" even though it's really just his desk and a server rack and a whiteboard covered in my handwriting from previous chapters of this disaster. His fingers are on the keyboard. He doesn't look up when I come in, but his shoulders shift the way they always do when he's tracking me from peripheral vision.
"Hey," he says.
"Hey."
"Good shoot?"
"Perfume ad."
"Ah."

He doesn't ask what that means. He already knows I have no idea what perfume smells like anymore, emotionally speaking. I know the top notes and base notes. I know the marketing brief. I know the target demographic. I know everything about it except why anyone would want to smell it.
I go to the balcony.

Our penthouse has this balcony — one of the reasons I fell in love with the place, back when I could fall in love with apartments. It faces west. Right now the sky is doing the second act of the same show from the rooftop, except this time I'm not working, not posing, not performing. It's just me and the fading light and the Manhattan skyline turning from gold to rust to bruise.

The wind is cool against my bare arms. I can smell the city — exhaust and rain and the particular metallic sweetness of New York in summer, like pennies in a storm drain. There's a taste in my mouth I can't identify. Metal, maybe. Or something like metal. The taste you get when you've lost something but your body hasn't figured out what yet.
I lean against the railing and I watch the sun sink.
And I try.
I really, really try.

I know this used to make me cry. I know it. I have the data: Chloe Hart, age eight, called her grandmother to describe the sunset from her bedroom window. Chloe Hart, age fourteen, wrote in her diary that sunsets were proof the universe had a soul. Chloe Hart, age twenty-one, sat on a fire escape in Brooklyn with her roommate and they both cried at the sky like it was a movie, and her roommate said, "How is this free?" and Chloe said, "Right? RIGHT?"
I know all of it. Every fact. Every detail.

But the feeling — the warm, sweet ache that used to bloom behind my sternum, the thing that made my throat tight and my eyes sting and my heart feel too big for my chest — it's not there. I'm checking the room and the furniture is gone. The walls are bare. The windows are shuttered.
The sunset is a JPEG. Beautiful and flat. High resolution, perfect color accuracy, zero emotional latency.
The last sliver of sun drops below the horizon. The sky goes from bruise to ash. I keep watching because I don't know what else to do.
The sliding glass door opens behind me. Leo steps out.
He doesn't say anything. He just stands next to me, close enough that our arms almost touch. The almost is everything. The almost is its own kind of gravity.

I can hear him breathing — slow, measured, the way he breathes when he's trying not to break something. The wind moves his hair. The city hums forty stories below, a sound like a machine that never stops being a machine.
I look at him. His cheeks are wet. Not sobbing — just wet. A shininess along his jaw, his eyes red-rimmed, his throat working like he's swallowing something sharp.
"Why are you crying?" I ask.
He shakes his head. He doesn't look at me. He's looking at the sky, that dead gray sky where the sunset used to be.
"You used to cry at sunsets," he says.
I turn back to the darkening horizon. The first stars are coming out. Or they might be satellites. Hard to tell anymore.
"That sounds like something I would do," I say.
Past tense.
Everything is past tense now.
I go inside and sit on the couch and try to feel nostalgic about something. Anything. I scroll through my own archive like a researcher combing through someone else's life.

My first apartment. 184 Havemeyer Street, Unit 3R. Williamsburg. $1,850 a month. One bedroom, one bathroom, a kitchen the size of a coffin. The faucet leaked. I reported it three times. Maintenance never came. I put a bucket under it and learned to sleep through the drip.

I remember all of it. Every fact. What I don't remember is the feeling of turning that key in the lock for the very first time. The pride. The small, fierce triumph of having my own space, my own door, my own leaky faucet. I know I felt it — I can find the Instagram post, the caption, the heart emojis from friends. But the feeling itself is behind glass now. I can see the shape of it. I can't touch it.

My first date with Leo. Il Porto, on Thompson Street. He wore a navy button-down with the sleeves rolled, the one with the fraying cuff. I had the rigatoni. He had the chicken scarpariello. He told me about his mom's cooking and I told him about my grandmother's and we talked for three hours and the waiter had to ask us to leave because they needed the table.

I remember the restaurant. I remember what he wore. I remember what we ate. I don't remember my heart racing. I don't remember the giddy nausea of a first date going well, that vertigo of thinking this person, this one, this could be something. I know I felt it. I can see the texts I sent to Kaia afterward — all caps, keysmash, seventeen exclamation points. But the feeling behind those exclamation points is a foreign language I used to speak.

Nostalgia was the connective tissue between my past and my present. It was the thing that made my history feel like mine instead of like a Wikipedia article I'd edited once. Without it, my past is just... data. A list of facts. An archive nobody visits. Dates and addresses and meal orders and lease agreements. Everything that happened and nothing that it meant.
The taste in my mouth is still there. Metal and something else. Something like the ghost of sweetness, if ghosts could have flavor.
I get up from the couch and go to the wall.

The wall of notes. It started as a small section above my desk — a few Post-its, a couple of scrawled reminders. Now it's spread across most of the living room wall like a serial killer's conspiracy board, except the conspiracy is me. Every deduction. Every loss. Written in my handwriting, in pen and marker and once, memorably, in lipstick when I couldn't find a pen at 3 a.m.
I pick up a pen from the desk drawer. A real one — a Pilot G2, black ink, 0.5mm. Not an Apple Pencil. Something that commits. Something that bleeds onto paper, that can't be undone with a tap.
I find a blank patch of wall between EMPATHY — GONE and TRUST — GONE and GRIEF — GONE and I write:
**NOSTALGIA — GONE. Can still access facts. Cannot access feelings about facts. The past is now a Wikipedia article about someone who used to live here.**
I underline it twice. The pen digs into the wall. I can feel the physical resistance, the drag of ink on paint. That I can feel. That registers.
I read it back. It's accurate. It's clinical. It's the most devastating thing I've ever written and I can't even feel devastated about it. The irony isn't lost on me. It's just... noted.
I tap the pen against my teeth. The taste of metal again — the ink, maybe, or whatever's left of my interior life.
Below everything, I write one more line:
**What's left when the memories can't remember how they felt?**
I stare at the question. The pen hangs in my hand. The ink is still wet. It shines in the lamplight like something alive, which is more than I can say for most of my emotional palette.

Leo's keyboard clicks in the other room. Steady, rhythmical, the sound of someone working late on something that matters. He's still chasing the self-repair mechanism. Still looking for the Catch-22 that could break Mephisto's hold. Still trying to save me with the kind of stubborn, quiet devotion that I can observe but can no longer ache for.

The city hums outside. A siren, distant. A helicopter, probably news. The endless white noise of eight million people living their lives with all their feelings still intact, crying at sunsets, calling their grandmothers, remembering how it felt to remember.
Four deductions. Empathy. Trust. Grief. Nostalgia. Four rooms emptied. Four lights switched off. Four shapes of things I used to hold, now just silhouettes on the wall.
Only love remains.

And somewhere in the architecture of this digital devil's bargain, Mephisto is already loading the next deduction. I can feel it the way you feel a train coming — not the sound, but the vibration in the platform, that subsonic tremor before the lights appear in the tunnel.
Love is loading. The last and only one left.
I set the pen down. I turn away from the wall. The question I wrote hangs behind me, drying in the apartment air:
*What's left when the memories can't remember how they felt?*
I don't have an answer.
I'm not sure I'd feel it if I did.
